stem: A confidence interval for a population mean uses critical value \( z = [[z]] \), known population standard deviation \( \sigma = [[sigmaval]] \), and sample size \( n = [[nval]] \). What is the margin of error \( z\cdot\dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \)?
solution: The margin of error is \( z\cdot\frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} = [[z]]\cdot\frac{[[sigmaval]]}{\sqrt{[[nval]]}} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the critical value \( z \) and reports only the standard error.
This divides by \( n \) instead of by \( \sqrt{n} \).
This uses \( \sigma \) directly without dividing by \( \sqrt{n} \) at all.
This multiplies by \( \sqrt{n} \) instead of dividing by it.

stem: A researcher wants the margin of error for a population mean to be at most \( E = [[E]] \), using critical value \( z = [[z]] \) and population standard deviation \( \sigma = [[sigma]] \). Using \( n = \left(\dfrac{z\sigma}{E}\right)^{2} \), what sample size \( n \) is required?
solution: Solving \( E = \dfrac{z\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \) for \( n \) gives \( n = \left(\dfrac{z\sigma}{E}\right)^2 = \left(\dfrac{[[z]]\cdot[[sigma]]}{[[E]]}\right)^2 = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to square the ratio \( z\sigma/E \).
This drops the critical value \( z \) from the ratio before squaring.
This squares only the numerator \( z\sigma \) and forgets to also square the denominator \( E \).
This inverts the ratio, dividing \( E \) by \( z\sigma \) instead of the other way around.
This is off by one from the correct sample size.

stem: A sample of size \( n = [[nval]] \) has sample mean \( \bar{x} = [[xbar]] \). The population standard deviation is \( \sigma = [[sigmaval]] \), and the critical value is \( z = [[z]] \). What is the upper limit of the confidence interval, \( \bar{x} + z\cdot\dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \)?
solution: The upper limit adds the margin of error to the sample mean: \( [[xbar]] + [[z]]\cdot\dfrac{[[sigmaval]]}{\sqrt{[[nval]]}} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This subtracts the margin of error, giving the LOWER limit instead of the upper limit.
This drops the critical value \( z \) from the margin of error.
This forgets to divide by \( \sqrt{n} \) when computing the margin of error.
This reports only the margin of error and forgets to add the sample mean.
This is off by one from the correct upper limit.

stem: A confidence interval for a population mean is reported as \( ([[lower]],\, [[upper]]) \). What is the margin of error for this interval?
solution: The margin of error is half the width of the interval: \( \dfrac{[[upper]] - [[lower]]}{2} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This reports the full width of the interval instead of half of it.
This computes the midpoint (sample mean) of the interval, not the margin of error.
This is just the upper endpoint of the interval.
This is just the lower endpoint of the interval.
This is off by one from the correct margin of error.
